title: Superseded fixed-monochrome C# and Python source views
summary: C# and Python retain their native source spelling and conventional language tooling while Tree-sitter and LSP semantic syntax colors are flattened; neither language participates in the C/C++/CUDA frontend.
details: C# is initially a language-learning surface, so its keywords, declarations, types, operators, strings, and punctuation remain spelled exactly as written rather than being translated through the C++ visual language.

Python follows the same ordinary-source policy. Both languages use Tree-sitter for structural parsing while language-specific highlight captures link to Normal; comments and Python docstrings remain dim.

LSP semantic tokens are disabled per C# and Python buffer because they are presentation metadata that would otherwise reintroduce the type/function/variable color categories the owner considers noise.

Roslyn owns C# language intelligence inside solution/project workspaces. BasedPyright owns Python language intelligence and defers project-specific type-checking policy to pyproject.toml or pyrightconfig.json.

The shared LspAttach layer supplies definition/reference/implementation/declaration navigation, rename, code actions, symbols, diagnostics, completion capability, document highlights, and optional inlay hints.
The C++ frontend and its Dans display controller remain scoped only to C, C++, and CUDA. Their state and character-changing renderers never apply to C# or Python.
rationale: Seeing the real C# grammar consistently is more useful while learning the language than immediately imposing a familiar but different C++ presentation vocabulary.
Flattening syntax categories preserves structural parsing and language intelligence without turning every token role into a competing visual signal.
A buffer-local semantic-token opt-out preserves every LSP request while making the monochrome promise robust after a server attaches.
alternatives: Reuse or adapt the C++ frontend for C#; deferred because C# has different syntax and semantics, and the owner explicitly wants to learn its native language first.
Use the colorscheme's default Tree-sitter and semantic-token palette; rejected because the owner considers the category coloring noise.
Disable Tree-sitter and language servers entirely to obtain monochrome text; rejected because parsing, navigation, diagnostics, refactoring, and completion are independently useful.

provenance.reference: conversation 2026-07-19 C# and Python support request
provenance.note: Owner requested C# language/LSP support while learning native C#, explicitly declined using the C++ frontend for now, selected monochrome over default coloring, and extended the same integration work to Python.

The pinned nvim-lspconfig supplies current roslyn_ls and basedpyright definitions; Mason registry names, Tree-sitter parser/query names, dependency requirements, and shared LspAttach behavior were verified before implementation.

evidence.claim: Real C# and Python parsers retain source bytes, flatten language captures, dim comments/docstrings, avoid every sampled C++ frontend namespace, disable semantic-token colors, and receive the shared navigation mappings.

aliases: do not use C++ frontend for C#
questions: Does C# use the Odin-like C++ frontend?
Why are C# and Python syntax colors flattened?
Can Roslyn and BasedPyright still navigate when semantic tokens are disabled?
Does the Dans monochrome toggle change Python presentation?

last_verified.method: Superseded by the owner's same-day clarification that C# and Python should default to monochrome but allow the Dans monochrome row to restore normal colors.
